data.title: Klarna chair Michael Moritz buys about $50M of shares
data.description:
• Klarna Group’s (KLAR) chairman Michael Moritz bought 3.47M ordinary shares worth about $50M through an associated entity between March 3 and March 11.
  • David Fock, the firm’s chief product & design officer, purchased 27,000 ordinary shares on March 9, 2026, for an aggregate consideration of $388,552.14.
  • Meanwhile, chief marketing officer David Sandström sold 32,703 shares on March 9, 2026, and chief commercial officer David Sykes sold 23,799 shares on March 13, 2026. Both sales were made pursuant to Rule 10b5-1 trading plans, established in 2025.
  • KLAR +7% premarket to $15.6.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: AM Markets Need to Know: Russian oil waivers, mineral trade deal, and more
data.description:
Stock index futures were mixed on Friday as investors prepared for a busy morning of economic data, with GDP and PCE figures topping the calendar.

Now, here are five news stories that broke overnight to watch out for:

Forced labor trade probe: The U.S. has launched trade investigations into 60 economies—including China, the European Union, Canada, Mexico, Japan, India and Russia—to determine whether they failed to ban imports of goods produced with forced labor. The investigations will be conducted under Section 301((b)) of the Trade Act of 1974, which enables the U.S. to impose tariffs on countries engaging in unfair practices that impact U.S. commerce.

Russian oil waivers: The United States has issued a 30-day waiver allowing countries to purchase sanctioned Russian oil and petroleum products already stranded at sea, expanding a temporary exemption granted last week to India alone. U.S. Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said in a post on X that this was a “narrowly tailored, short-term measure” that applies only to oil already in transit.

ByteDance AI expansion: TikTok’s Chinese parent ByteDance (BDNCE) is assembling computing power with top Nvidia (NVDA) chips outside China, the Wall Street Journal reported on Thursday. ByteDance is working with Southeast Asian firm Aolani Cloud to deploy about 500 Nvidia Blackwell computing systems in Malaysia, totaling roughly 36,000 B200 chips.

Mineral trade agreement: The U.S., Japan, and the European Union are set to announce plans in the coming weeks to lay the foundation for a trade agreement in critical minerals, Bloomberg News reported. The Office of the U.S. Trade Representative, which has led negotiations with Brussels and Tokyo on the framework, will also head talks for a trade deal that is set to include a price floor and tariffs for the materials to counter any market distortions by China.

Amazon Prime Day timing: Amazon (AMZN) plans to shift its annual Prime Day sale to late June from July, sources told Bloomberg. A timing change would have broad implications across the retail sector as thousands of third-party vendors and competitors such as Walmart (WMT), Target (TGT), Costco (COST) and Best Buy (BBY) consider whether they need to change their summer schedules.

data.title: PAR Technology stock dips after pricing $250M convertible debt offering
data.description:
PAR Technology (PAR) has priced a private offering of $250M aggregate principal amount of 4.00% Convertible Senior Notes due 2031.

The company also granted to the initial purchasers of the notes a 13-day option to purchase up to an additional $15M aggregate principal amount of notes. The sale is expected to close on March 17, 2026, subject to satisfaction of the conditions to closing.

The notes will mature on March 15, 2031, unless earlier converted, redeemed, or repurchased. Interest will accrue on the notes at 4.00% per year.

The initial conversion rate will be 52.5762 shares of the common stock per $1,000 principal amount of notes (equivalent to an initial conversion price of $19.02 per share of the common stock, which represents a conversion premium of 20.0% above the last reported sale price of the common stock on the New York Stock Exchange on March 12, 2026).

The company expects to receive about $242.3M in net proceeds from the offering (or about $256.8M if the option to purchase additional notes is fully exercised), after deducting discounts, commissions, and offering expenses.

It plans to use the proceeds to repurchase about $207.5M of its 1.50% Convertible Senior Notes due 2027, buy back roughly 2.09M shares of common stock for about $33.1M, and allocate the remaining funds for general corporate purposes.

PAR shares fell -20% premarket to $12.64 on Friday.

data.title: PagerDuty slips on soft revenue outlook, churn concerns despite Q4 beat
data.description:
Shares of PagerDuty (PD) slipped 13.5% in Friday's premarket trading despite reporting a quarterly beat, as the company issued a weaker-than-expected revenue forecast. Analysts also pointed to a high churn rate and softness in paid user licenses.

The company forecasts first-quarter revenue in the range of $118 million to $120 million, compared to the average analyst estimate of $123.5 million. Adjusted EPS is expected to be between $0.23 and $0.25 vs. estimate of $0.28.

Analysts raised concerns about the sustainability of retention improvements, implications of pricing transitions, and the potential for flat revenue growth, with a tone best characterized as neutral to slightly cautious.

Morgan Stanley analyst Sanjit Singh rates the stock an equal weight and cut the PT to $7 from $9, arguing that growth is pressured by compression in paid user licenses, weaker gross retention, and mid-market churn, Bloomberg reported. PagerDuty (PD) also reported 4Q annual recurring revenue that missed estimates.

Meanwhile, RBC Capital Markets analyst Matthew Hedberg gives the stock a "sector perform" rating, trimming PT to $8 from $9. While PagerDuty reported a better-than-feared quarter with AI native/enterprise customer acceleration, there were continued headwinds related to paid user licenses, the brokerage said, according to media reports. RBC remains “cautious around macro pressures as the stock remains a show-me story.”

The stock is down 45% year to date and carries a Strong Sell rating from the SA Quant system, while Wall Street analysts rate it a Buy.

data.title: América Móvil unit eyes Azteca, Total Play fiber assets
data.description:
Mexico's América Móvil (AMX) considers buying telecom assets from Columbia-based Grupo Salinas, the conglomerate founded by Ricardo Salinas Pliego.

Comcel, a subsidiary of América Móvil (AMX) that operates under the Claro brand in Colombia, has requested authorization from the regulatory body to integrate strategic assets owned by Azteca Comunicaciones Colombia and Total Play Comunicaciones Colombia, a local newspaper reported.

The operation includes the acquisition of the so-called National Fiber Optic Project Network and an approximate extension of 20,000 kilometers, as well as the ACC Network of about 12,000 kilometers, both operated by Azteca Comunicaciones, a company of the Salinas Group.

The transaction also includes the transfer of contracts for the use and management of the fiber optic network, the contractual position in a commercial trust for administration and payments, as well as assets such as infrastructure, towers, and lease agreements. No valuation details are disclosed yet.

Salinas Pliego, the owner of Grupo Salinas, is monetizing assets following a $1.9B tax settlement with Mexico's government. If completed, the acquisition would deepen América Móvil’s footprint in Colombia.

data.title: New York City weighs raising minimum wage to $30 by 2030
data.description:
Remember the "Fight for $15" that started in New York City last decade? Well, the movement has evolved and the goalposts have moved, with "$30 by '30" being demanded in the Big Apple. Mayor Zohran Mamdani, a democratic socialist, already advocated for the new minimum wage level on the campaign trail, but the pledge is now being taken up at the New York City Council.

Rallying cry: If approved, the newly introduced bill would stagger wage increases over the next several years, bringing the minimum wage to "$30 an hour by 2030" - for businesses with over 500 workers. Smaller employers would have two additional years until 2032, with any further increases tied to cost-of-living adjustments. It's a big deal. The current minimum wage in NYC is $17, and at $30, it would be the highest city minimum wage in the country.

According to NYC's Comptroller, one quarter of the local workforce earns minimum wage, meaning an increase would impact over a million workers. The proposed bill also aims to phase out the tip credit for food service workers, while implementing robust reporting requirements. Any failure to maintain or produce wage records would create rebuttable presumptions of the employer, which must provide formal notice to employees about their rights on an annual basis.

Outlook: "The math ain't mathing," said Brooklyn City Council Member Sandy Nurse, who put forward the "30 for Our City" Act. "$17 per hour is not a livable wage. It is a crisis. This is not a dignified life." Others argue that such a sharp increase could deepen monopolies and pressure small businesses, which are already facing sky-high rent and tight profit margins. Additional fears center around higher prices due to increased labor costs, as well as an increase in the unemployment rate, especially among low-skilled workers.

data.title: Kyivstar Group jumps after posting double-digit revenue growth
data.description:
• Kyivstar Group (KYIV) announced FY25 revenue rose 25.9% Y/Y to $1.16B; 4Q25 revenue increased 28.4% Y/Y to $321M.
  • EBITDA for FY25 climbed 25.8% Y/Y to $648M, with a margin of 56.0%.
  • Adjusted net profit reached $289M, with EPS at $1.32, beating the consensus estimate of $1.19 by $0.13.
  • Unadjusted net profit for 4Q25 was $90M.
  • Digital revenue jumped 4.7x Y/Y to $124M, accounting for 10.7% of total revenue, reflecting strong growth in the company’s digital ecosystem.
  • Outlook: Kyivstar Group (KYIV) expects 2026 USD revenue growth of 8%–11% and EBITDA growth of 5%–8%, assuming UAH/USD at 44.5; capex intensity is expected at 23%–26% of revenue.
  • CEO Oleksandr Komarov said, “We enter 2026 with strong momentum, supported by accelerating revenue growth. In 4Q25, digital revenue expanded more than sixfold, reaching nearly 16% of total revenue. Our 30% Y/Y revenue growth in hryvnia reflects successful execution of our digital expansion roadmap and deeper group-wide synergies, with meaningful contributions across business verticals.”
  • Kyivstar Group (KYIV) shares rose around 4.9% to $10.70 in Thursday premarket hours.

data.title: STMicroelectronics plans to deploy robots, retrain workers: report
data.description:
On Thursday, STMicroelectronics (STM) unveiled ​plans to retrain workers and deploy robots in its ‌older chipmaking facilities, avoiding closures, as the chipmaker navigates industry challenges, Reuters reported.

At a semiconductor conference hosted by industry group SEMI in Sopot, Poland, Thomas Morgenstern, STMicroelectronics' head of manufacturing, showed ​a video of a robot placing a silicon wafer carrier into a ​machine, the report added.

"This is the first one we have," said Morgenstern. "In the next couple ⁠of years, we are talking about numbers beyond one hundred humanoids doing jobs ​in our facilities."

STMicroelectronics did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.

Morgenstern ​noted that humanoids would take over repetitive and physically demanding tasks, ​allowing workers ⁠to move into higher-skilled roles that are in short supply. He said the company had started a training initiative to align workforce skills with emerging needs, the report noted.

"If you have a ⁠three or ​four-shift system, one humanoid can replace three out ​of four shifts," Morgenstern said to the news outlet. "We don't want to close any facility in Europe…the goal is to ​increase efficiency."

European chipmakers, including STMicroelectronics and competitors like NXP Semiconductors (NXPI) are facing pressure ​from global rivals, mainly in China, where modern automated production lines are increasing efficiency.

Older fabs, or chipmaking facilities, need large investments to compete, but they are often not upgradeable with newer tools. Options ​to dismantle and rebuild are complicated by high costs, regulatory hurdles, and negotiations ​with unions in Europe, the report added.

Older fabs are generally not eligible for EU Chips Act funding, as subsidies are geared towards "first-of-a-kind" projects. However, industry groups, including SEMI, are pushing for more investments in supply chains and existing industrial strengths as part of a refreshed Chips Act 2.0, the report noted.

STMicroelectronics, in which the French and Italian governments hold a stake, has been carrying out a restructuring plan announced in October 2024, which includes proposed layoffs. Progress has been made in France, but the efforts face hurdles in Italy.

data.title: European indexes slip with no signs of de-escalation in Middle East conflict
data.description:
London (UKX) -0.55% to 10,248. UK economy stagnates in January as 0% GDP growth misses forecasts.

Germany (DAX:IND) -0.83% to 23,393.

France (CAC:IND) -0.91% to 7,911. France inflation rises to 0.9% in February.

In other parts of Europe, Spain's inflation remains stable at 2.3%, as expected.

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) fell 0.80% to 593.5, with still no signs of de-escalation in the Middle East conflict. Consumer cyclicals, consumer defensives, and financials were among the worst-performing sectors. The euro extended its recent decline, falling below $1.15 to its weakest level since late July, as the US dollar gained broadly amid escalating tensions in the Middle East. 

In the bond market, the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield was almost flat at 4.27%. 

Germany's 10-year yield was up 1 basis point to 2.96%.

UK's 10-year yield was up less than 1 basis point to 4.78%.

Currencies: (EUR:USD) (GBP:USD) (CHF:USD)

ETFs: (NYSEARCA:EWG), (NYSE:GF), (NYSEARCA:EWI), (NYSEARCA:EWQ), (NASDAQ:FGM), (NASDAQ:DAX), (NYSEARCA:FLGR), (NYSEARCA:FXB), (NYSEARCA:EWU), (NASDAQ:FKU), (BATS:EWUS), (NYSEARCA:FLGB), (NYSEARCA:GREK)

data.title: Nasdaq, S&P, Dow futures tread cautiously ahead of PCE, GDP
data.description:
Stock index futures were mixed on Friday as investors prepared for a busy morning of economic data, with GDP and PCE figures topping the calendar.

Dow futures (INDU) edged higher by +0.016%. S&P 500 futures (SPX) slipped -0.0096%, and Nasdaq 100 futures (US100:IND) fell -0.0757%.

Durable goods orders and GDP will land at the same time Wednesday morning. Orders are expected to rise 0.5% after falling 1.4% on weak aircraft orders a month earlier, while the consensus sees no revision for GDP.

Personal income and outlays will also be released simultaneously, with income expected to outpace spending—income up 0.5% and spending up 0.3% on the month.

U.S. Treasury yields ticked higher across the curve. The 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) stood at 3.75%, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose to 4.27%, and the 30-year Treasury yield (US30Y) climbed to 4.90%.

Top gainers premarket included Darden Restaurants (DRI) +5.66%, Mosaic (MOS) +3.64%, and CF Industries (CF) +3.04%. Decliners included Ulta Beauty (ULTA) -8.92%, Adobe (ADBE) -8.71% and Insulet (PODD) -3.91%.
